coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical synthesis of Ni doped carbon quantum dots for simultaneous fluorometric determination of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ion facilely
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communication Co. Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communication Co. Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Siyuan
Sun, Yang
Yang, Fan
Che, Sai
Zhang, Xiaoyun
Zhang, Ge
Li, Yongfeng
coredata.dc:description: A novel Ni doped carbon quantum dots (Ni-CQDs) fluorescence probe was synthesized by facile electrolysis of monoatomic Ni dispersed porous carbon (Ni–N–C). The obtained Ni-CQDs showed a high quantum yield of 6.3% with the strongest excitation and emission peaks of 360 nm and 460 nm, and maintained over 90% of the maximum fluorescence intensity in a wide pH range of 3–12. The metal ions detectability of Ni-CQDs was enhanced by Ni doping and functional groups modification, and the rapid and selective detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions was achieved with Ni-CQDs through dynamic and static quenching mechanism, respectively. On one hand, the energy band gap of Ni-CQDs was regulated by Ni doping, so that excited electrons in Ni-CQDs were able to transfer to Fe3+ easily. On the other hand, the abundant functional groups promoted the generation of static quenching complexation between Cu2+ and Ni-CQDs. In metal ions detection, the linear quantitation range of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were 100–1000 μM (R2 = 0.9955) and 300–900 μM (R2 = 0.9978), respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) were calculated as 10.17 and 7.88 μM, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence quenched by Cu2+ could be recovered by EDTA2− due to the destruction of the static quenching complexation. In this way, Ni-CQDs showed the ability to identify the two metal ions to a certain degree under the condition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ coexistent. This work paves the way of facile multiple metal ion detection with high sensitivity.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrolysis
Ni doped CQDs
Fe3+ detection
Cu2+ detection
Ions distinction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Band gap and molecular orbital energy level measurements
Selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+
Fluorescence recovery detection and metal ions distinction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image of Ni-CQDs. (b) Height of selected Ni-CQDs. (c) Height distribution of Ni-CQDs. (d) and (e) TEM images of Ni-CQDs at different magnifications.
The UV–Vis spectra (a) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra (b) of Ni-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of Ni–N–C and Ni-CQDs. (b) The XPS spectra of Ni-CQDs and spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) Ni 2p.
The selectivity of Ni-CQDs to different (a) metal ions and (b) anions. The relationship between F/F 0 and concentration of (c) Fe3+ and (d) Cu2+.
The energy level simulation of Ni-CQDs.
Fluorescence recovery of Ni-CQDs quenched by Cu2+ and Fe3+ with EDTA2−.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical synthesis of Ni doped carbon quantum dots for simultaneous fluorometric determination of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ion facilely

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel Ni doped carbon quantum dots (Ni-CQDs) fluorescence probe was synthesized by facile electrolysis of monoatomic Ni dispersed porous carbon (Ni–N–C). The obtained Ni-CQDs showed a high quantum yield of 6.3% with the strongest excitation and emission peaks of 360 nm and 460 nm, and maintained over 90% of the maximum fluorescence intensity in a wide pH range of 3–12. The metal ions detectability of Ni-CQDs was enhanced by Ni doping and functional groups modification, and the rapid and selective detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions was achieved with Ni-CQDs through dynamic and static quenching mechanism, respectively. On one hand, the energy band gap of Ni-CQDs was regulated by Ni doping, so that excited electrons in Ni-CQDs were able to transfer to Fe3+ easily. On the other hand, the abundant functional groups promoted the generation of static quenching complexation between Cu2+ and Ni-CQDs. In metal ions detection, the linear quantitation range of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were 100–1000 μM (R2 = 0.9955) and 300–900 μM (R2 = 0.9978), respectively. The limits of detection (LOD) were calculated as 10.17 and 7.88 μM, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence quenched by Cu2+ could be recovered by EDTA2− due to the destruction of the static quenching complexation. In this way, Ni-CQDs showed the ability to identify the two metal ions to a certain degree under the condition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ coexistent. This work paves the way of facile multiple metal ion detection with high sensitivity.

Ni-CQDs with NiNx doped and functional groups adjusted has capacity in detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions simultaneously and facilely. Based on different detection mechanism, the two ions can be distinguished by EDTA2− through fluorescence recovery.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Copper and iron as essential trace elements for human, but surplus intake causes serious health concerns such as kidney problems, pancreas diseases, and cancers [1–4]. With extensive application in industry and agriculture, the toxicities of copper and iron pollution in water and soil pose serious threats to human health. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect the concentration of copper and iron ions. Although conventional analytical techniques such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5] and atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [6] have been widely applied in metal ions detection, high cost of instruments and poor portability dramatically limit their applications [7]. Hence, it is necessary to exploit copper and iron ions detection techniques with convenience and accuracy.

Nowadays, semiconductor quantum dot fluorescence probes have been widely used in metal ions detection for their convenient process and accurate result [8–10]. However, they are usually limited by complicated synthetic process and high toxicity [11]. As a new kind of fluorescent probes, carbon quantum dots overcome these deficiencies and have been widely applied in metal ions detection including Fe3+ [12], Hg2+ [13], Cu2+ [14], Al3+ [15], Pb+ [16], Zn+ [17], and so on. Although the relationship between structure and performance still remains unclear, these fluorescent probes present great performance in their corresponding detection fields based on different mechanisms including static quenching [18] or dynamic quenching [19]. In static quenching, the complexation of metal ions and ground state carbon quantum dots quenches the fluorescence [18]. In dynamic quenching, the fluorescence probe in excited state forms the energy complex with metal ions by molecular collision, which quenches the fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [19]. Based on static quenching or dynamic quenching detection mechanisms, these fluorescence probes are usually sensitive to only one kind of metal ion. However, circumstances exist where several metal ions need to be detected at once, so fluorescence probes with multiple metal ions detection capability are of great significance for high-efficiency practical applications. In recent researches, several CQDs based probes have been used in multiple metal ions presence detection to distinguish different ions [20]. On one hand, some specific chelating agents were used before the detection process to mask one metal ion, so that the other ion could be detected separately [21,22]. For instance, ascorbic acid or trisodium citrate was used in Hg2+ and Cu2+ discrimination [23]. However, in this way, the ability to differentiate metal ions relies on the pretreatment of chelating agents, and the detection process is complicated. On the other hand, CQDs probe with response to metal ions in both fluorescence spectrum and absorption spectrum can differentiate individual metal ions via discrepant affect in absorption spectrum [24]. For instance, Bandi and his co-workers [24] reported a novel N-doped carbon quantum dot for Fe3+ and Cu2+ detection, where Fe3+ increased absorption at about 320 nm in absorption spectrum, but Cu2+ had the opposite effect. However, the detailed quenching mechanism of carbon quantum dot-based fluorescence probe remains ambiguous, and the extra UV absorption spectrum determination complicates the detection process. In this regard, it is crucial yet challenging to synthesize fluorescence probes with convenient multiple metal ions detection ability. Based on the mechanism of static and dynamic quenching, the fluorescence is quenched by the different non-fluorescent complexes with different stabilities [18,19]. Afterwards, the fluorescence can be recovered by the destruction of these non-fluorescent complexes. Hence, with deliberate selection of fluorescence restorer, a probe with both static and dynamic detection mechanism will possess the ability of simultaneous multiple metal ions detection with simplified process. In our previous study [25], the energy band gap of CQDs could be regulated effectively by metal doping, thus adjusting the selectivity in dynamic quenching. In addition, the formation of static quenching complexation was affected by functional groups such as hydroxyl and amino groups on probe surface. Therefore, it is meaningful to synthesize novel CQDs modified with metal doped and functional groups for simultaneous and facile multiple metal ions detection.

In this work, with a top-down method, a novel Ni doped carbon quantum dots (Ni-CQDs) was synthesized by the electrolysis of monoatomic Ni dispersed porous carbon. With the effect of electrochemical etching [26], Ni-CQDs was etched from Ni dispersed porous carbon, which made Ni doped in Ni-CQDs. Ni-CQDs-based fluorescence probe was applied in Fe3+ and Cu2+ detection. Fe3+ detection was based on the dynamic quenching mechanism proved by fluorescence lifetime detection. The linear quantitation range of Fe3+ was from 100 to 1000 μM (R2 = 0.9955) with a limits of detection (LOD) of 10.17 μM. As for Cu2+ detection, the linear quantitation range of Cu2+ was from 300 to 900 μM (R2 = 0.9978) with a LOD of 7.88 μM, which was attributed to static quenching mechanism. Moreover, Ni-CQDs maintained over 90% of the maximum fluorescence in pH range from 3 to 12. More importantly, quenched fluorescence could be recovered with the addition of EDTA2−, due to the destruction of the complexation of Cu2+ and Ni-CQDs. Therefore, Ni-CQDs identified two ions to a certain degree under the condition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ coexistence.

Analytical grade of BaCl2, CaCl2, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, ZnCl2, CdCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3, NiCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CuCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, NaBr, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaHPO4, NaNO3, Na2SO4, NaOH, MgO, 1,10-phenanthroline, polytetrafluoroethylene preparation (PTFE, 60 wt%), and spectral grade of ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Corporation (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared by deionized (DI) water and chemicals were used without further purification.

The monoatomic Ni dispersed porous carbon (Ni–N–C) was synthesized according to the MgO template methods in our previous research [27]. The Ni-CQDs was synthesized in electrolytic method, which electrode was made by mixture of Ni–N–C and PTFE coating on the carbon cloth [25]. The Ni–N–C (25.5 mg) and PTFE (4.5 μL) were mixed in moderate ethanol and the slurry was coated on the carbon cloth (3 cm × 3 cm) as the anode. The electrolyte was composed of 30 mg NaOH, 8 mL H2O and 35 mL C2H5OH and the Pt foil was used as counter electrode. With electrolysis under the voltage of 5 V for 24 h, yellow electrolyte was collected and dialyzed over DI water in a dialysis bag (3500 Da). Finally, the Ni-CQDs powder was obtained after freeze drying process.

The Ni-CQDs powder was dissolved to 1 mg/mL by DI water, and excitation wavelength was set as 360 nm. Fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution were recorded as F, which contained different metal ions and anions solutions (0.5 mL, 1 mM) with Ni-CQDs suspension (0.5 mL, 1 mg/mL). The fluorescence intensity of Ni-CQDs suspension with concentration of 0.5 mg/mL was set as initial fluorescence intensity. Specially, a drop of HCl (0.1 M) was added into above suspension to inhibit the hydrolysis. Different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were used in sensitive detection experiment. All the above experiments were carried out under the same conditions at room temperature. LOD were calculated based on 3σ/K, where σ was the standard deviation of F/F 0. F 0 was the initial fluorescence and F was the fluorescence quenched by metal ion with different concentrations. K was slope, which belonged to linear fitting line of the F/F 0 versus concentration of metal ion. The pH stable detection was carried out similarly with different pH solutions adding, which solutions were adjusted by HCl and NaOH.

The particle size and morphology were observed by an atomic force microscope (Bruker Multimode 8). The property of ultraviolet–visible absorption was measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2700). The property of fluorescence was measured by fluorescence detector (Hitachi F7000), and the fluorescence lifetime was measured by FLS-9800. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) were measured by Thermo Fisher K-Alpha spectrometer with an exciting source of Al Kα. The property of Fourier transform infrared absorption (FT-IR) was measured by a Bruker Tensor II. The concentration of Ni was analyzed by ICP-OES (Optima 7300 V).

The quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as reference with QY of 0.54 at 360 nm. And the QY of Ni-GQDs was measured according to the following equation.
(1)
φ x
=
φ st
K x
/
K st
η x
/
η st
2
Where “x” and “st” stand for Ni-CQDs sample and quinine sulfate, respectively. “Ф” is the quantum yield and “K” is slope belongs to linear fitting line of the fluorescence intensity integral area versus absorbance. η is the solution refractive index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap and molecular orbital energy level measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
The band gap of Ni-GQDs was estimated according to the following equation.
(2)
A h ν
K '
1 / 2
= h ν −
E g
Where, A is the ultraviolet–visible absorption. The hv is 1240/wavelength and K' is the product of sample concentration and thickness of the cuvette. Here, the sample concentration was 0.5 mol/L and the thick of cuvette was 1 cm. E g is the band gap.

The molecular orbital energy level was estimated according to the following equations.
(3)
E LUMO = -e(E Red ​+ ​4.4)
(4)
E HOMO = E LUMO - E g
Where, E LUMO is the lowest unfilled molecular orbital energy level and E HOMO is the highest filled molecular orbital energy level. E Red is the value of reduction potential measured by voltammetry.
As shown in Scheme 1 , the Ni-CQDs were synthesized successfully with a top-down method by electrolyzing Ni–N–C. The Ni-CQDs powder was obtained from electrolyte by dialyzed and freeze drying.

In order to analyze the morphology, AFM image of Ni-CQDs in solution was obtained as shown in Fig. 1 a. The synthesized Ni-CQDs had quasi-spherical structure with uniform size, which were well separated from each other. The granulometric analyze of selected Ni-CQDs and all particles (Fig. 1b and c) showed that the particles size of Ni-CQDs distributed mainly in a section from 4.25 nm to 5.00 nm. As shown in Fig. 1d and e, the TEM image of Ni-CQDs also showed the same result of the particles size, which had a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon (insert in Fig. 1e) [28].

The UV–Vis spectrum of Ni-CQDs (Fig. 2 a) exhibited a remarkable absorption peak at about 210 ​nm, with a hump at around 280 ​nm. These two peaks can be rooted in the π-π∗ electronic transition of CC bond [29] and n-π∗ electronic transition of CO and CN bonds [30], respectively. As shown in fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2b), with a gradual hike of excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 410 nm, there is a red shift in the emission wavelength and the emission intensity showed a typical excitation-dependent feature with a maximum emission wavelength at 460 nm. With using quinoline sulfate as reference (QY = 54%), the QY of the Ni-CQDs in water was calculated to be 6.3% as shown in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence of Ni-CQDs was stable in pH value from 3 to 12, which had over 90% of the maximum fluorescence. The above results indicated that Ni-CQDs with stable fluorescence was successfully synthesized by this top-down method.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the chemical structure change between Ni–N–C and Ni-CQDs was investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy. Both Ni–N–C and Ni-CQDs possessed absorption peaks of O–H/N–H stretching vibration (around 3403 cm−1), C–H stretching vibration (around 2935 cm−1), C–N stretching vibration (around 1629 cm−1) and C–O stretching vibration (around 1113 cm−1). On the other hand, new absorption peaks of CC/CN stretching vibration (around 1930 cm−1) was observed due to the formation of defect in Ni-CQDs. The formation of CO group (around 1674 cm−1), N–O group (around 1386 cm−1), C–O–C group (around 1048 cm−1) [25] and the increase of O–H group illustrated that the content of oxygen-containing functional group in Ni-CQDs increased significantly. These results implied that abundant functional groups including hydroxide, carboxyl, nitro, and amino were generated on the Ni-CQDs surface, which enhanced the stability in aqueous system and increased the number of chelating sites.

XPS was further performed for Ni-CQDs structural elucidation. As shown in Fig. 3b, there were four typical peaks of C 1s (283 eV), N 1s (405 eV), O 1s (530 eV), and Ni 2p (866 eV), respectively. The high-resolution region of C 1s (Fig. 3c) consisted four characteristic peaks of CC, C–N, C–O and CO structure located at 284.6, 286.2, 287.8 and 289.3 eV, respectively. The high-resolution region of N 1s (Fig. 3d) showed that five peaks at 398.5, 399.6, 400.6, 403, and 407 eV were assigned to pyridinic N, Ni–N, pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and oxidized N, respectively [31,32]. The existence of Ni–N bonds illustrated that Ni atom was doped in Ni-CQDs successfully in the structure of Ni–N. In addition, compared to Ni–N–C [26], the composition percentage of oxidized N was at a high level, which was due to oxidization in electrolysis etching process and in agreement with the FT-IR results. In the high-resolution region of O 1s (Fig. 3e), there were three peaks located at 531.3, 532.4, and 535.7 eV, which were assigned to structure of CO, C–O–C/C–O–H, and N–O, respectively. It is consistent with the results of C 1s and N 1s spectrums that oxygen-containing functional groups gathered on the Ni-CQDs surface. The high-resolution region of Ni 2P spectrum showed weak signal of Ni on account of low Ni content (Fig. 3f), which demonstrated the doping of Ni in Ni-CQ and was in agreement with the results of N 1s spectrum. Furthermore, as shown in Table S1, the content of Ni was measured by ICP-OES, which reconfirmed that Ni atom was doped successfully with 0.23 wt%. These results suggested that a kind of Ni dope CQDs with abundant surface functional groups was synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the detectability of Ni-CQDs, fluorescence quenching comparative experiment was performed, which metal cations of Ba2+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and anions of Br−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2− were set as potential detection target. As shown in Fig. 4a and 4b, the fluorescence of Ni-CQDs quenched by Fe3+ and Cu2+, while fluorescence quenching caused by other metal cations and anions can be ignored with over 90% fluorescence intensity. These results confirmed the selectivity of Ni-CQDs to Fe3+ and Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 4 c and d, a great detection linearity range from 100 to 1000 μM (R2 = 0.9955) for Fe3+ with a LOD of 10.17 μM and from 300 to 900 μM (R2 = 0.9978) for Cu2+ with a LOD of 7.88 μM illustrated the well linear detection detectability for Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. Compared with relevant reported works shown in Table S2, Ni-CQDs possessed good detection ability of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in a wide range under a high concentration. These results indicated that Ni-CQDs possessed great selectivity and sensitivity in Fe3+ or Cu2+ ion detection.

The fluorescence lifetime detection was performed to investigate the mechanism of Ni-CQDs fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, the lifetime of fluorescence decay decreased from 3.933 ns to 3.720 ns after addition of Fe3+, which kept stable with the Cu2+ addition. These results illustrated that fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ was due to dynamics quenching, which excited state electrons of Ni-CQDs transferred to the empty 3d orbitals of Fe3+ and energy complex was formed [33]. On the other hand, with the addition of Cu2+, complexation of Cu2+ and ground state Ni-CQDs was formed, which the fluorescence was quenched in static quenching mechanism. These results proved that Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions can be detected by Ni-CQDs in different detection mechanism. The possible mechanism of selectivity is proposed to further explain by energy level simulation. Compared with our previous work [25], the doped Ni shrunk the band gap of Ni-CQDs to 3.14 eV (Fig. S4), which overlapped the lattice field stabilization energy of Fe3+. At the same time, the LUMO of the Ni-CQD was calculated as −4.24 eV, which was approximate to the Fe3+. Therefore, the electrons in LUMO of Ni-CQDs could transfer easily to vacant electron orbital of Fe3+, which quenched the Ni-CQDs by dynamics quenching (Fig. 5 ) [24,34]. Meanwhile, the result of fluorescence lifetime had a small difference value between original lifetime and addition of Fe3+ or Cu2+, which illustrated a potential inner filter effect existed in detection process. A part of fluorescence excited from Ni-CQDs was absorbed by metal ions. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of Ni-CQDs may be caused by both dynamics/static quenching mechanism and inner filter effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery detection and metal ions distinction
ce:section.ce:para: As proved by fluorescence lifetime detection, the fluorescence quenching of Ni-CQDs caused by Fe3+ and Cu2+ was due to different mechanism of dynamics quenching and static quenching respectively, which afforded the possibility to distinguish the metal ions existence simultaneously. To detect the separating capacity, fluorescence recovery experiment was carried out with C2O4 2-, NH4Cl, EDTA2-, NH4F and PO4 3- (1 mM) as potential fluorescent restorers. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of Ni-CQDs quenched by Cu2+ was recovered obviously, which was recovered to maximum extent over 90% by EDTA2− (Fig. S5a). On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity quenched by Fe3+ had little recovery with the addition of fluorescent restorers. These results illustrated that EDTA2− could be used as fluorescent restorer to distinguish the Fe3+ and Cu2+ by fluorescence recovery. It might be due to that the complex of EDTA2− and Cu2+ generated and no fluorescence complexation of Cu2+ and Ni-CQDs was broken, which the fluorescence of Ni-CQDs was recovered. However, complex of EDTA2− and Fe3+ had no effect on the energy complex of Fe3+ and Ni-CQDs generated by molecular collision, which the fluorescence of Ni-CQDs had no recovery.

Furthermore, the separating capacity of EDTA2− in different concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+ was detected as shown in Fig. S6. With the different concentration of Fe3+ and Cu2+ from 100 to 1000 μM, the fluorescence intensity quenched by Fe3+ had little recovery but fluorescence intensity quenched by Cu2+ had obvious stable recovery about 90%. These results illustrated that EDTA2− had stable separating capacity of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in different concentrations, which afforded the possibility in detection under the condition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ existed at the same time.

As shown in Fig. 6 , fluorescence recovery experiment quenched by Fe3+ and Cu2+ at same time was carried out with the concentration of Fe3+ at 0.5 mM and the concentration of Cu2+ at 0.5 and 1.0 mM, respectively. With quenched adequately by Fe3+, fluorescence intensity of Ni-CQDs was further quenched by Cu2+ to a low level. Whereafter, the fluorescence was recovered by EDTA2− to the about 90% of the F 0 ’ (fluorescence quenched by Fe3+), which illustrated that EDTA2− had separating capacity of Fe3+ and Cu2+ under the condition of existence at the same time to some degree. In assumptions process, the concentration of Cu2+ could be calculated by recovered fluorescence intensity firstly, and the concentration of Fe3+ could be calculated by detection linearity equation and fluorescence recovery coefficient (fluorescence recovered by EDTA2− about 90%).

As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S3, fluorescence recovery experiment was carried out in a larger concentration range. However, it was a pity that the concentration calculated by the method mentioned in assumptions process was unsatisfactory, while the fluorescence intensity was recovered obviously. With the low fluorescence quantum yield, the concentration of Ni-CQDs should at a high level to meet the detection requirement, which would affect the accuracy of detection in low metal ion concentration. For all this, metal ions distinction by Ni-CQDs based on different quenching mechanism still had great inspiration in multiple metal ions detection.

In conclusion, a novel Ni doped carbon quantum dots was successfully synthesized through top-down electrolysis, and the corresponding fluorescence probe was applied in selective detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+. The band gap of Ni-CQDs decreased by Ni doping, promoting the Fe3+ detection based on dynamic quenching. With energy level simulation, the doping of Ni was revealed to adjust the LUMO of Ni-CQDs (−4.24 eV) approximate to that of the Fe3+. Meanwhile, the abundant functional groups such as hydroxide, carboxyl, nitro, and amino groups contributed to the detection of Cu2+ based on static quenching. The identification of Fe3+ and Cu2+ could be completed in wide linear quantitation range of concentrations of 100–1000 μM and 300–900 μM, respectively. Due to different quenching mechanisms, EDTA2− was further applied as fluorescence restorer to distinguish the Fe3+ and Cu2+ by fluorescence recovery. With the addition of EDTA2−, non-fluorescent complexation of Cu2+ and Ni-CQDs was destructed, and the fluorescence recovered. In contrast, EDTA2− had no effect on the Fe3+/Ni-CQDs complex. Therefore, EDTA2− recovered the fluorescence quenched by Cu2+, but showed little influence on the fluorescence quenched by Fe3+. Although this fluorescence recovery applied to a narrow concentration range, we envision that metal ions distinction using different quenching mechanisms brings great inspiration in the application of multiple metal ions detection.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully thank for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21776302 and 21776308), and the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (No. 2462020YXZZ033).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gce.2022.05.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper binding to the N-terminal tandem repeat regions of mammalian and Avian prion protein

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.P. Hornshaw, J.R. McDermott, J.M. Candy, Copper Binding to the N-Terminal Tandem Repeat Regions of Mammalian and Avian Prion Protein, Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 207 (1995) 621-629.
S.S. Percival, Copper and immunity, Am. J. Clin. Nutr. 67 (1998) 1064-1068.

Y. Wang, L. Yu, J. Ding, Y. Chen, Iron Metabolism in Cancer, Int. J. Mol. Sci. 20 (2018) 95-117.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon dots originated from Lycii Fructus for effective fluorescent sensing of ferric ion and multicolor cell imaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of lead in blood by square wave anodic stripping voltammetry at a carbon disk ultramicroelectrode

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from Lantana camara berries for effective detection of lead(II) and bioimaging
